text:
Riverside Neurology Clinic

Clinic Date: 14 September 2025

Dr Patel
Riverside Health Centre
22 Brook Street, Manchester M3 4AB

Dear Dr Patel,

James Morton, DOB: 12-07-1987, Hospital No: M847520, NHS No: 5039227146
Flat 3, 18 Lawson Court, Manchester M4 5HS

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures with prior head injury; cognitive slowing noted by family; vitamin D deficiency on treatment; past variability in community pharmacy supply with intermittent adherence; lives independently with weekly carer check-ins; former alcohol use now in remission; non-smoker.

Current medication:
- Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily
- Carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily
- Topiramate 50 mg morning and 100 mg evening
- Cholecalciferol 800 IU daily; Thiamine 100 mg daily; Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly; Lansoprazole 15 mg daily; Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily; Ramipril 5 mg daily; Emollients as required

History since last review:
I saw James Morton in clinic today accompanied by his sister, who helps organise prescriptions. Since our previous appointment six months ago, there have been no emergency attendances. He reports improved routine with pill dosing after moving to a weekly blister pack, though he and his sister describe occasional delays obtaining medication when the pharmacy has been out of stock, echoing the documented past variability in supply.

Seizure history:
- He describes a clear pattern around his partner’s recent childbirth three months ago: there was a noticeable spike in events in the weeks surrounding the birth, with two brief episodes of behavioural arrest and lip-smacking reported by his sister, each under one minute, without injury or hospitalisation. Since that period, frequency has settled back to baseline with only one possible brief event last month.
- No convulsive events, no tongue biting, and no prolonged post-ictal confusion reported this year.
- Triggers noted: sleep disruption during the newborn period and missed evening doses on two occasions when collection of repeat medication was delayed.

Examination:
Alert and oriented. Speech fluent, mild word-finding hesitation. Cranial nerves intact. Power full in all limbs, tone normal, coordination intact, gait stable. No focal neurological deficits.

Investigations:
- Bloods March 2025: U&E, LFT, FBC within reference; vitamin D low-normal on supplementation.
- No recent imaging required. ECG July 2025 normal sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms.

Assessment:
- Seizure disorder with recent transient increase temporally related to major life change and sleep disruption surrounding his partner’s childbirth, now returned towards baseline.
- Historical adherence impacted at times by pharmacy supply variability; current blister pack system helpful but occasional stock gaps persist.

Plan:
1) Continue current antiseizure regimen unchanged.
2) Reinforce adherence strategies: maintain weekly blister packs; sister to request repeats 10–14 days before due date to mitigate stock issues; pharmacy to be contacted to align collection dates for all medications.
3) Provide written seizure first-aid and safety advice to family, with emphasis on sleep hygiene during newborn care.
4) Arrange blood monitoring (U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D) in 3 months via practice; earlier if clinical concern.
5) Seizure diary to be kept; if events exceed baseline again, contact clinic for expedited review.
6) Routine follow-up in 6 months, sooner if frequency worsens or injuries occur.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: there was a noticeable spike in events in the weeks surrounding the birth, with two brief episodes... Since that period, frequency has settled back to baseline with only one possible brief event last month.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes recent seizure activity. It states there was a spike around the partner’s childbirth three months ago with two brief episodes in the weeks surrounding the birth, and since then only one possible brief event last month. The instruction is to select the highest frequency when multiple exist. The spike period implies 2 events over a few weeks; however, an exact per-time unit rate is not numerically specified (no precise count per week or month given). Currently, he is not seizure-free for 6 months (last possible event was last month). Because no explicit numeric frequency per standardized time unit can be determined from the text, and the exact rate during the spike period cannot be converted into an allowed format confidently, the frequency is considered unknown.
seizure_frequency_number: there was a noticeable spike in events in the weeks surrounding the birth, with two brief episodes... Since that period, frequency has settled back to baseline with only one possible brief event last month.
